articles.title: RBA Payments Board Lifts No-Surcharge Ban, Eyes System-Wide Crypto Uplift
articles.summary: The Reserve Bank of Australia's Payments System Board, meeting on 4 June 2026, confirmed implementation of its Review of Merchant Card Payment Costs and Surcharging and flagged a sweeping new consultation on payments regulation that will reach far beyond card networks. Following the Conclusions Paper published on 31 March 2026, the RBA amended RBA Standards Nos 1, 2 and 3 to give effect to the review's conclusions, with the amended standards registered on the Federal Register of Legislation on 14 April 2026. The Board confirmed it will lift the prohibition on no-surcharge rules for the designated eftpos, Mastercard and Visa networks, and the RBA has been engaging designated card networks on plans to introduce no-surcharge rules. Australian Payments Plus published an update the same day confirming a zero-surcharge limit will apply to eftpos transactions from 1 October 2026. The Board also flagged a public consultation commencing by the end of June to seek stakeholder views on issues warranting regulatory intervention and how they should be prioritised — covering competition, efficiency and safety issues including mobile wallets, three-party schemes such as American Express, BNPL providers and e-commerce platforms. Separately, the Board discussed emerging risks from advances in classical and quantum computing and agreed to broaden the RBA's focus to system-wide cryptographic issues across the Australian payments system, extending beyond card payments, reaffirming the December 2030 target for quantum-related cryptographic uplift. The Board also approved its full assessment of RITS, Australia's real-time gross settlement system, against the Principles for Financial Market Infrastructures. For Airwallex — whose founding market is Australia and which operates as an acquirer and PSP locally — the changes carry direct product and pricing implications. The interchange-reduction and scheme-fee transparency measures affect Airwallex's cost base and merchant pricing models, while the impending end of surcharging reshapes how merchant clients structure card acceptance. The mid-2026 consultation extending to e-commerce platforms, mobile wallets and BNPL signals broadening regulatory perimeter that a product lawyer should engage with through submissions, and the cryptographic uplift mandate creates a forward compliance obligation for Airwallex's card-handling infrastructure.

articles.title: Airwallex Acquires Leapfin to Build End-to-End Financial Lifecycle Platform
articles.summary: Airwallex announced on 4 June 2026 that it has acquired Leapfin, a financial data automation platform specialising in revenue recognition and reconciliation, in a move that extends the global payments company's reach from accepting payments through to closing the books. The acquisition is designed to allow businesses to transition operational transaction data into GAAP-ready financials, integrating Leapfin's revenue-accounting capabilities with Airwallex's global infrastructure to give finance teams a foundation for the entire record-to-report process. CEO and co-founder Jack Zhang said finance teams are managing unprecedented complexity across multiple entities, currencies and fragmented data systems, and that bringing Leapfin's data engine and accounting AI agents into the Airwallex ecosystem lets the company close the loop on the financial lifecycle from accepting payments to closing the books. Airwallex said its infrastructure processes over $266 billion in annual transaction volume and serves more than 250,000 customers globally, and that Leapfin's core product, leadership and engineering teams will continue operating to ensure continuity for existing customers. The deal follows Airwallex's September acquisition of OpenPay and its May 2026 launch of Airwallex Billing, signalling a deliberate push up the financial-operations stack against rivals such as Stripe Billing and Recurly. Airwallex noted it holds 85+ licences across North America, Europe, the Middle East and Asia-Pacific. For a product lawyer, the acquisition raises several action items: integrating Leapfin's revenue-recognition and accounting-AI functionality may engage data-governance, customer-data-use and AI-transparency obligations across multiple jurisdictions, particularly as EU AI Act high-risk classifications and AMLA-era data integrity expectations tighten. Diligence on Leapfin's existing customer contracts, data-processing arrangements and any regulated-activity exposure is critical, as is confirming that the combined record-to-report product does not stray into regulated bookkeeping or audit territory in key markets. The deal also reinforces Airwallex's competitive positioning as a full-stack financial platform rather than a pure payments provider — a strategic shift legal teams should map against the company's existing licensing footprint.

articles.title: Zip Co Limited v Firstmac Limited [2026] HCA 16
articles.summary: Facts: Firstmac, a non-bank lender, registered the word mark 'ZIP' in 2004 for financial services (loans) in Class 36. From November 2013 the Zip Companies (Zip Co and Zipmoney), operating buy-now-pay-later services, used signs containing 'ZIP' that were substantially identical or deceptively similar to the Firstmac Mark. Before launching, the Zip Companies had received adverse IP Australia examination reports citing the Firstmac Mark but proceeded without seeking legal advice. Issue: Whether the Zip Companies could rely on the 'honest concurrent use' defences under ss 122(1)(f) and 122(1)(fa), read with s 44(3), of the Trade Marks Act 1995 (Cth), and the relevant date and meaning of 'honesty' for assessing that defence. Holding: The Court unanimously dismissed the appeal, holding that honest concurrent use is assessed at the time of each alleged potential infringement (not by reference to later-built reputation), and that 'honesty' is judged against the standard of ordinary, decent people. Having ignored a known conflict, the Zip Companies could not prove honesty at first use. Significance: For commercial and fintech practitioners, the decision is a cautionary tale on brand clearance: a fast-growing fintech can face injunctions, damages and forced rebranding despite substantial later goodwill. It clarifies that an adverse examination report is a formal warning, and that businesses must conduct clearance searches, document their reasoning, and address conflicts before commencing use.
